title: Using publicly writable directories is security-sensitive
description: This rule raises an issue whenever it detects a hard-coded path to a publicly writable directory like /tmp (see examples bellow). It also detects access to environment variables that point to publicly writable directories, e.g., TMP, TMPDIR and TEMP. %USERPROFILE%\AppData\Local\Temp Files are read from or written into a publicly writable folder The application creates files with predictable names into a publicly writable folder There is a risk if you answered yes to any of those questions.
recommendation: Review and fix according to the rule guidelines.

checks.message: Using publicly writable directories is security-sensitive. Use Path.GetTempPath() or Environment.SpecialFolder instead of hardcoded temp paths

logic.detection_conditions: Hardcoded path to publicly writable directory or access to temp environment variables
logic.triggering_conditions: OR: (1) String literal containing /tmp/, /var/tmp/, C:\Windows\Temp\ OR (2) GetEnvironmentVariable call for TMP, TEMP, or TMPDIR
logic.reasoning: Publicly writable directories like /tmp and C:\Windows\Temp are accessible by all users on the system. Using hardcoded paths to these directories creates security risks including: symlink attacks, file prediction attacks, and race conditions. Use Path.GetTempPath() which returns the user's temp directory, or Environment.SpecialFolder for application-specific storage.
